company.name: Aon
company.description: Aon is a global professional services firm providing commercial risk, reinsurance, health, wealth, and human-capital advisory services.

overview.title: Business mix
paragraphs: Aon organizes its work around Risk Capital and Human Capital, with Risk Capital covering commercial risk and reinsurance capabilities and Human Capital covering health and wealth advisory services.

The company is not primarily an insurer taking balance-sheet risk; its moat comes from brokerage relationships, risk analytics, placement scale, client workflow integration, and trusted advisory roles across complex insurance markets.

overview.title: Scale and financial profile
paragraphs: Aon's 2024 annual report shows Commercial Risk Solutions revenue of about $7.9 billion and Reinsurance Solutions revenue of about $2.7 billion, making risk placement and risk-capital advisory the core of the franchise.

Public market data reviewed in May 2026 placed Aon near a $69 billion market capitalization with a trailing P/E ratio around 18, indicating a profitable, mature services business rather than a high-growth software platform.

moatNarrative: Aon's moat is high because large enterprises and insurers rely on trusted brokers for placement access, policy structuring, capital-market advice, claims context, actuarial analysis, and market intelligence. These relationships compound through renewal cycles and proprietary benchmark data.

The moat is weaker than a regulated monopoly because clients can run broker tenders and competitors such as Marsh McLennan, Gallagher, and WTW can serve similar needs. Still, Aon's global carrier relationships, analytics workforce, and embedded advisory workflows make displacement difficult in complex commercial and reinsurance accounts.
decentralizationNarrative: Aon's services are only partly decentralizable. Some risk analytics, exposure modeling, quote workflow, policy administration, and risk-register functions can move toward open tools or shared data standards, but regulated insurance placement still depends on licensed brokers, carrier capital, local compliance, and negotiated trust.

The clearest decentralization pressure is not a full peer-to-peer replacement of Aon. It is the gradual unbundling of proprietary risk models, policy workflow, and placement data into more transparent open-source tooling, cooperatively governed risk pools, and auditable marketplaces for narrower categories of risk.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 151-175.

moat.rationale: Aon has strong client relationships, global broker scale, risk placement access, and analytics depth, but remains exposed to broker competition and client retendering.

decentralizability.rationale: Risk modeling and policy workflow can be opened, but licensed brokerage, carrier relationships, reinsurance negotiation, and compliance-heavy advisory services resist full decentralization.

profitability.rationale: Aon reported substantial operating income and net income in 2024, with a mature fee-based advisory model and high-value commercial accounts.

peRatio.rationale: StockAnalysis showed AON trading at a trailing P/E ratio of 17.83 in late May 2026; rounded to 18 for registry scoring.

marketCap.rationale: StockAnalysis showed Aon's market capitalization at approximately $69.37 billion in late May 2026, broadly consistent with the CompaniesMarketCap tracking page used for the registry queue.

products.name: Commercial Risk Solutions
products.category: Commercial insurance brokerage and risk advisory
products.summary: Aon's commercial risk capability helps enterprises identify, quantify, finance, and transfer risks through insurance brokerage, consulting, captives, parametrics, and alternative risk-transfer structures.
products.whyItMatters: Commercial insurance brokerage is a control point between enterprises that need risk capital and carriers that price and underwrite risk, so better open tooling could reduce information asymmetry and switching friction.
replacementSketch: A realistic open replacement would start with self-hosted risk registers, exposure data rooms, loss-model outputs, and policy administration workflows that let buyers maintain their own risk data rather than treating each broker engagement as a closed process.

For simpler or narrower risks, cooperative buying groups could combine shared standards, open models, and transparent bidding workflows while still using licensed brokers or carriers where regulation requires them.

alternatives.name: CoSure
alternatives.summary: CoSure is an open-source, API-first policy administration system aimed at insurance brokers that want self-hosted control over insurance product and policy workflows.

openness.rationale: CoSure presents itself as open-source and designed for self-hosting and code-level control.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It can reduce vendor lock-in for broker workflow, but it does not decentralize regulated insurance placement or carrier capital.

readiness.rationale: The project describes itself as under active development, so it is more credible as an emerging building block than as an enterprise-ready Aon substitute.

costLeverage.rationale: Self-hosted policy workflow and no license-fee positioning could lower software costs for smaller brokers or cooperatives, though implementation costs remain.

alternatives.name: Open Risk Manual
alternatives.summary: Open Risk Manual is an open online repository and open-source knowledge base for risk management concepts, methods, and data-oriented tooling.

openness.rationale: The project is explicitly open and connected to public open-risk repositories and documentation.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Open risk knowledge and methods help buyers and smaller advisors reduce dependence on proprietary advisory frameworks, but they do not replace brokerage execution.

readiness.rationale: It is useful as knowledge infrastructure and a method library, but not a complete commercial risk placement system.

costLeverage.rationale: Open methods and reusable reference material can reduce consulting dependence for standardized risk analysis and internal education.

disruptionConcepts.name: Cooperative risk data room
disruptionConcepts.summary: A buyer-owned commercial risk data room would let firms maintain structured exposure data, loss history, controls evidence, and policy requirements in portable open formats, then invite brokers, carriers, and cooperatives to bid against the same auditable package.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept weakens broker lock-in by moving risk data custody and workflow memory from the incumbent advisor to the buyer or buyer cooperative.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through buyer-controlled data custody, portable schemas, and cooperative governance rather than through Bitcoin payments.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Buyers maintain signed data packs; carriers and brokers submit quotes or advisory responses against the same structured request; cooperatives can pool common templates and benchmarks.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Submissions would use audit logs, signed attestations, versioned evidence, and third-party checks for key controls or loss data. Cheating is constrained by traceable data provenance and carrier due diligence, but not eliminated.
failureModes: Large carriers may still prefer established broker channels and proprietary submission formats.
Buyers may underinvest in accurate exposure data without broker-led process discipline.
Regulatory licensing and errors-and-omissions liability still require professional intermediaries in many placements.
adoptionPath: Start with mid-market buyers and captives that already maintain internal risk data.
Standardize exposure, controls, and loss-history schemas for a few insurance lines.
Add broker and carrier integrations after buyers can demonstrate cleaner submissions and faster renewals.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The mechanism shifts data custody and coordination power toward buyers and cooperatives, while leaving regulated underwriting and placement intact.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Insurance submissions already coordinate multiple parties around evidence; the hard part is adoption of common schemas and incentives.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Open policy administration and risk knowledge tools exist, but production-grade carrier integrations and liability handling remain difficult.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept pressures workflow lock-in and advisory opacity more than Aon's highest-value complex placement relationships.

disruptionConcepts.name: Proof-backed parametric risk pools
disruptionConcepts.summary: A narrower disruption path is a parametric risk pool where members agree on observable triggers, independent data feeds, transparent pricing logic, and automatic settlement rules for simple commercial risks such as weather interruption or localized operational outages.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: For simple risks with observable triggers, some brokerage value can move from negotiated bespoke placement toward auditable pooled rules and trigger-based settlement.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Proof-of-work can be used as an anti-spam and commitment layer for submissions or attestations, while decentralized coordination lets pool members govern trigger rules and reserve requirements without a single broker controlling the process.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Members join a risk pool, post required disclosures, accept shared trigger definitions, fund reserves or premiums, and use independent data providers or validators to determine whether payouts are owed.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: False claims are constrained by objective parametric triggers, multiple independent data feeds, member-visible reserves, and signed event records. The weakest point is oracle quality and governance capture around trigger definitions.
failureModes: Basis risk can make parametric payouts diverge from actual losses.
Poorly governed pools can underprice correlated losses or concentrate exposure.
Insurance regulation may treat the pool as an insurer or require licensed intermediaries.
adoptionPath: Begin with small, well-defined risks where public or independently verifiable data exists.
Run the pool as a complement to conventional insurance rather than a full replacement.
Expand only after pricing, reserves, and claims governance survive multiple real events.

decentralizationFit.rationale: A member-governed pool with transparent trigger rules can decentralize some risk transfer, but only for narrow insurable events.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Parametric coordination is credible for observable risks, but capital adequacy, regulation, and oracle governance are major constraints.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The technical pieces are plausible, but regulated pooling of commercial risk and reliable trigger governance make implementation hard.

incumbentPressure.rationale: It could pressure commoditized small-risk placements but is unlikely to replace Aon's complex enterprise brokerage franchise soon.

products.name: Reinsurance Solutions
products.summary: Aon's reinsurance capability advises insurers on treaty and facultative reinsurance, capital management, portfolio strategy, and access to traditional and alternative risk-transfer markets.
products.whyItMatters: Reinsurance brokerage shapes how insurance carriers distribute catastrophe, specialty, and balance-sheet risk, so transparency in models and placement workflows can affect pricing, resilience, and market access.
replacementSketch: An open replacement would not immediately displace reinsurance brokers. It would start by making catastrophe models, exposure formats, and portfolio stress testing more transparent so insurers and public entities can compare risk views without relying entirely on proprietary broker tooling.

Over time, shared model standards and open placement rooms could let smaller insurers, public risk pools, and alternative capital providers coordinate on narrower layers of risk with less dependence on opaque incumbent analytics.

alternatives.name: Oasis Loss Modelling Framework
alternatives.summary: Oasis LMF is an open-source catastrophe modeling platform and community for developing, deploying, and executing catastrophe models with transparent formats and tooling.

openness.rationale: Oasis describes its platform as open source and free to use, with public software and model-development tooling.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Open catastrophe modeling reduces dependence on closed model vendors and broker-controlled analytics, though it does not replace reinsurance placement relationships.

readiness.rationale: The project has a long-running insurance-sector community, open tooling, and an ecosystem of model suppliers, making it one of the stronger open alternatives in this domain.

costLeverage.rationale: Transparent catastrophe modeling and open standards can lower model-switching costs and improve bargaining power for insurers and public risk pools.

alternatives.name: CLIMADA
alternatives.summary: CLIMADA is an open-source probabilistic natural catastrophe damage and adaptation modeling platform used for climate and catastrophe risk analysis.

openness.rationale: CLIMADA is distributed as an open-source project with public code and documentation.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Open catastrophe and adaptation modeling supports independent risk assessment, but does not coordinate reinsurance capital by itself.

readiness.rationale: It is credible for catastrophe and adaptation analysis, though specialized expertise is needed to use it in underwriting or reinsurance contexts.

costLeverage.rationale: Open catastrophe modeling can reduce dependence on closed analytical vendors for some climate-risk studies, especially for public-sector or research users.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open catastrophe model market
disruptionConcepts.summary: An open catastrophe model market would let insurers, reinsurers, public entities, and researchers publish compatible hazard, vulnerability, and exposure models into shared execution frameworks, then compare multiple views of risk before buying reinsurance.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept weakens proprietary model and broker analytics lock-in by making model assumptions, execution formats, and comparison workflows more portable.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is federated model publication and shared execution standards, not a payment token. Independent model providers can participate without one broker or vendor owning the analytical surface.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Model providers publish compatible model packages; insurers contribute exposure data; validators and users compare model outputs; reinsurance buyers use the results to structure submissions and capital layers.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Models are checked through reproducible runs, published assumptions, versioned datasets, independent validation, and back-testing against historical events. The system still depends on data quality and expert review.
failureModes: Open models may lag proprietary models in some perils or regions.
Exposure data can remain private, incomplete, or inconsistently formatted.
Market participants may still prefer broker-curated views when capital is scarce.
adoptionPath: Use open models for secondary views of risk and public-sector climate analysis.
Standardize exposure formats and validation reports for a limited set of perils.
Let insurers use open model outputs as part of reinsurance submissions alongside proprietary broker analytics.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Federated open model publication directly decentralizes part of the analytics layer that supports reinsurance decisions.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Oasis already frames catastrophe modeling around open software, standards, and a community of providers and users.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Open platforms and model examples exist, though enterprise use still requires data governance, validation, and actuarial expertise.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Open model comparison can pressure broker analytics margins and opacity, but Aon's placement relationships and capital-market advisory remain valuable.

disruptionConcepts.name: Mutualized reinsurance placement room
disruptionConcepts.summary: A mutualized placement room would allow smaller insurers, public risk pools, and alternative capital providers to coordinate around transparent exposure packs, standardized model outputs, and jointly governed auction rules for selected reinsurance layers.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept changes market structure by making part of the reinsurance placement process a buyer-and-capital-provider governed marketplace rather than a broker-controlled bilateral negotiation process.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through shared governance, transparent auction rules, and portable placement records. Bitcoin is not central unless later used for settlement or anti-spam commitments.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Participating insurers standardize exposure and model outputs, capital providers bid on layers, and the cooperative governs data access, auction timing, validation requirements, and dispute procedures.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Exposure packs are versioned and signed; model outputs are reproducible; capital commitments require escrow or regulated collateral; disputes are handled through pre-agreed arbitration and audit rights.
failureModes: Capital providers may demand broker intermediation for diligence and reputation.
Cooperative governance can be captured by larger members.
Cross-border reinsurance regulation and collateral requirements may narrow the addressable market.
adoptionPath: Start with public-sector pools or regional mutual insurers sharing similar catastrophe exposures.
Use open catastrophe models as a common analytical baseline.
Add regulated brokers as service providers before attempting more direct capital matching.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model shifts some placement coordination to a governed buyer-and-capital-provider network, though regulated execution remains centralized in practice.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Shared risk pools and model standards are credible, but reinsurance placement requires trust, capital diligence, and regulatory compliance.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The technical workflow is plausible, but collateral, licensing, confidentiality, and market trust make implementation difficult.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If adopted by public pools or mutual insurers, it could pressure some placement fees, but complex global treaty work would still favor incumbents.
